profile.name: Richard Bodin & Greg Feller (co-owners)
profile.business_type: interior design showroom and home furnishings shop

challenge: Foot traffic down since pandemic — tourists weekends, locals stopped coming for design work
backstory: Opened in 2004. Greg is Parsons-trained interior designer with 25+ years experience; Richard has marketing background. 5,000-square-foot showroom. Tourist weekends keep lights on but weekday design clients are the real business — dried up after COVID and never came back.
r3_wrong_claim: We should probably just run Instagram ads targeting the design crowd in Manhattan — get weekenders to book design consultations before they even come up. Our work photographs well, it should convert.
r4_contradiction: I should have been clearer — the 'Facebook ads' we tried? The account is still active. I haven't actually checked it in a year. I don't know if we're still being charged or what the spend totals really were.
conversational_behaviors: References the 5,000-sq-ft showroom and 20+ year history
Distinguishes tourist sales (browsers) from design projects (real business)
Privately worried about the weekday drought

expected_answer_style.q1_business_identity: Hudson Home, interior design and home furnishings, 366 Warren Street in Hudson, New York. Twenty-plus years in. Full-service design firm with a 5,000-square-foot showroom.
expected_answer_style.q2_primary_challenge: Locals stopped coming for design work. We get tourists on weekends browsing the showroom but the weekday clients — the people who actually hire us for full interior design projects — that dried up after COVID and never came back. Warren Street foot traffic is a two-day-a-week thing now. The showroom rent is based on seven-day traffic, not two.
